content.full_text:
PLEASE REPLY TO

SACRAMENTO ADDRESS
STATE CAPITOL
SACRAMENTO 95814
916—445 7210

OR

SAN DIEGO ADDRESS
267 HORNBLEND
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92102
714—270 5800

LAWRENCE KAPILOFF
ADMINISTRATIVE ASSISTANT

Assembly
California Legislature

STANDING COMMITTEES
VICE CHAIRMAN WATER PARKS
AND WILDLIFE
RESOURCES, LAND USE
AND ENERGY
REVENUE AND TAXATION

LAWRENCE KAPILOFF
ASSEMBLYMAN, SEVENTY-EIGHTH DISTRICT

CHAIRMAN
SUBCOMMITTEE ON LAND USE

March 28, 1978

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA    95814

ATTN: Mr. Robert Stern, General Counsel

Dear Mr. Stern:

Enclosed, please find a copy of correspondence from Mr.
Marshall E. Conner relative to financial disclosures.

I would greatly appreciate receiving a copy of your reply
to Mr. Conner inasmuch as I am concerned about the im-
plications raised in his letter.

Thank you in advance for your attention to this matter.

Sincerely,

LAWRENCE KAPILOFF

LK:smj

Enclosure

Assemblyman Larry Kapiloff
967 Hornblend St.
San Diego, Ca. 92109


Dear Mr. Kapiloff:

Attached is a copy of my letter directed to The Fair Political Practices Commission. The purpose of this letter was to clarify the position of the Bill Reform Act of '74 as they relate to my position as "Auditor" with the State of California, Department of Benefit Payments.

Article 4 of the Bill of Rights from the Constitution of the United States should provide some protection from unreasonable searches. When I am required by law to file a report of my financial dealings, I believe that my position with the State should be protected in a substantial manner. The definitions of the Political Reform Act are vague and the law should be amended to show the specific types of income and investments, if any, that must be reported.

I trust that you will take notice to remedy the inequities of this law. In order that you are afforded by the provisions of the two you are also required to submit, a statement of your own income and investments. A copy of the Political Reform Act of 1974 and subsequent amendments would be informative and helpful. Thank you very much for your assistance and cooperation.

Sincerely Yours,
[signature] Marshall E. Corman
"Auditor" B. Corman

Marshall E. Conner
4425 - 30th St.
San Diego, CA 92116

February 16, 1978


Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 207
Sacramento, CA 95804

RE: Opinion Request

Gentlemen:

I am an employee of The Department of Benefit Payments, Employment Tax Office. My official title is Tax Auditor III, and as such, my department's Conflict of Interest Code Stipulates that I am required to file form Gen. 700, "Statement of Economic Interests."

Definitions of reportable income and investments under the Political Reform Act of 1974, Government Code Section 81000 et. seq., are at best vague and misleading. As you know, my department's Conflict of Interest Code has the force of law and any violation of it's provisions may subject me to the sanctions provided by law. Since a substantial question of interpretation is involved, I hereby request a formal written opinion on the questions raised in the next paragraph.

The following questions are pertinent to my immediate concerns:

1. Does the use of credit cards issued by banks and various department stores create reportable income under Sec. 87103 of the Government Code?

2. Are contributions to the State of California - Deferred Compensation Plan offering shares in no load mutual funds from T. Rowe Price Growth Stock Fund, Inc., reportable as income under Sec. 87103 of the Government Code?

3. Do loans from banks or credit unions obtained to purchase household furnishings or to purchase a car constitute reportable income?

4. Are accumulated contributions in the Public Employees Retirement System considered to be reportable as an investment?

5. Is interest on the accumulated contributions in the Public Employees Retirement System considered to be an item of reportable income?

6. Does the obtaining of a loan from any bank, credit union, or savings and loan association to purchase a private residence create a reportable income or investment item?

7. Are my "investments and income received" from any financial institution as defined in the California Financial Privacy Act (Government Code Sections 7465 - 7493) protected from disclosure to Fair Political Practices Commission or to the Personnel Bureau of the Department of Benefit Payments?

Fair Political Practices                -2-           February 16, 1978
Commission


     It is my opinion that Government Code Section 7470 pre-
vents any request for information contained in the finan-
cial records of any customer from a financial institution.
I believe that Government Code Section 7470 and 27307 are
in conflict.

8.  Government Code Section 81008 provides that no conditions
shall be imposed upon anyone with a desire to inspect my
"Statement of Economic Interest." However, I feel that
the Governor's Executive Order B-22-76 Section 3 provides
some protection from disclosure of my file. Is my "State-
ment of Economic Interest" protected by Order B-22-76?

     I request your formal written opinion as soon as possible.
If I do not receive a written opinion on each of the above points
please provide all necessary information needed to file an appeal
of any denial of my opinion request. Thank you very much for your
prompt attention to my request.


                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Marshall E. Conner

cc: Governor Edmund G. Brown, Jr.
    State Capitol Building
    Sacramento, CA  95814

    Assemblyman Larry Kapiloff
    967 Hornblend St.
    San Diego, CA  92109

    Senator Bob Wilson
    2165 San Diego Ave., Suite 202
    San Diego, CA  92110

sections.question_synthetic: Marshall E. Conner, a Tax Auditor III, seeks clarification on what types of financial interests and income are reportable under the Political Reform Act of 1974, including questions about credit cards, deferred compensation plans, loans, retirement accounts, and privacy protections under the California Financial Privacy Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is not an FPPC response letter, but rather an incoming request from a citizen. The document includes three separate letters: (1) Kapiloff's cover letter to FPPC requesting a copy of the response, (2) Conner's February 21 cover letter to Kapiloff, and (3) Conner's February 16 formal opinion request to FPPC with 8 numbered questions. No FPPC conclusion or response is present in this document. Document classification is challenging due to lack of traditional section headers and the multi-letter format.

I would greatly appreciate receiving a copy of your reply to Mr. Conner inasmuch as I am concerned about the implications raised in his letter. Thank you in advance for your attention to this matter. The purpose of that letter was to clarify the provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974 as they relate to my position as the Auditor III with the State of California, Department of Benefit Payments, Employment Tax Office. The Political Reform Act of 1974 requires me to disclose my financial affairs solely because I am employed by the State of California. Article 4 of the Bill of Rights from the Constitution of the United States should provide some protection from unreasonable searches. When I am required by law to file a report of my financial dealings, I believe that my individual right to privacy has been abridged in a substantial manner. The definitions of the Political Reform Act are vague and the law should be amended to show the specific types of income and investments, if any, that should be reported. I trust that you will take action to remedy the inequities of this law. I am aware that you are also affected by the provisions of this law since you are also required to submit a statement of your own income and investments. A copy of the Political Reform Act of 1974 and subsequent amendments would be informative and helpful. Thank you very much for your assistance and cooperation. Sincerely Yours, Marshall E. embedding.summary: This document contains an incoming request to FPPC from Marshall E. Conner (a state employee) asking for a formal written opinion on eight specific questions regarding reportable income and investments under the Political Reform Act of 1974, transmitted through Assemblyman Lawrence Kapiloff who is requesting a copy of FPPC's response.
